H-OS-L1.name: Pebble
H-OS-L1.sphere: H-OS-L1 (Pebble, n=73, mean=6.55, std=1.80, strong confidence) is the largest single-protocol sample in the dataset and provides the most stable estimate in the Over-stimulation subcategory. Distribution is approximately normal with a modest right skew; qualitative data (n=27) shows 'Driving / commuting' and 'Just listening' as joint top activities, suggesting a heterogeneous use context that plausibly contributes to the moderate std. Rhythm ratings split evenly between 'Balanced / comfortable' and 'Very settling / calming', indicating the L1 architecture is landing across a broad listener range.
H-OS-L1.sphere_plain: Pebble has the largest listener base of any protocol in the dataset, which makes its results particularly meaningful. It's working broadly well for a wide range of people using it in all kinds of situations — at home, commuting, caregiving. The spread of results is moderate, reflecting that different people in different contexts get different things from it. A reliable, well-evidenced foundation protocol.

H-OS-L2.name: Rainfall
H-OS-L2.sphere: H-OS-L2 (Rainfall, n=58, mean=6.71, std=1.85, strong confidence) outperforms L1 by mean (+0.16), with qualitative data (n=20) showing 'Noticeable improvement' as the dominant change rating (10/20) and a 10-minute modal settle time — the longest in the OS subcategory. The higher settle time at L2 is consistent with the wing-ratio architecture extending entrainment duration at longer protocol lengths.
H-OS-L2.sphere_plain: Rainfall improves on Pebble's mean score and shows consistently positive improvement reports from listeners. It takes a bit longer to settle into — around 10 minutes typically — which makes sense for a longer protocol. The rhythm is landing well across the board, with most people finding it either comfortably balanced or notably calming. A strong mid-tier protocol.

H-OS-L3.name: Glacier
H-OS-L3.sphere: H-OS-L3 (Glacier, n=44, mean=6.98, std=1.78, meaningful confidence) is the strongest performer in the OS subcategory by mean, with qualitative data (n=23) revealing 'Trying to sleep' as the top activity (8/23) — the highest sleep-use rate for a non-Sleep protocol. This cross-purpose usage is worth flagging: listeners may be self-routing to Glacier for sleep rather than over-stimulation recovery, which could be inflating or deflating scores depending on how well the DESCENT architecture serves sleep onset.
H-OS-L3.sphere_plain: Glacier scores highest in the over-stimulation group and has a pleasant surprise: a large number of people are using it to try to sleep, even though it's designed for over-stimulation recovery. It seems to be working reasonably well in that context too, which suggests the descent architecture is genuinely flexible. Worth noting in routing guidance as a secondary sleep option.

H-AX-L1.name: Meadow
H-AX-L1.sphere: H-AX-L1 (Meadow, n=65, mean=7.14, std=1.89, strong confidence) is the highest-performing protocol in the HEALING series by mean score, with pct_pos at 62% and a right-skewed distribution peaking at scores 6–9. Qualitative context (n=23) shows a substantial caregiver/clinician proxy presence (10/23) and a modal settle time of ~10 minutes, with rhythm predominantly rated 'Balanced / comfortable' — consistent with a well-calibrated DESCENT onset for a general anxiety population.
H-AX-L1.sphere_plain: Meadow is the top performer in the Healing series — more people report feeling better from it than almost any other protocol, and the improvement tends to feel real and noticeable rather than marginal. People using it on behalf of someone else (caregivers, therapists) make up a significant chunk of responses, and most say it felt comfortable rather than overwhelming. A solid, reliable starting point for anxiety and stress.

H-AX-L2.name: Cottage
H-AX-L2.sphere: H-AX-L2 (Cottage, n=51, mean=6.90, std=1.63, strong confidence) shows a tighter distribution than L1 with slightly lower std, suggesting more consistent — if marginally lower ceiling — outcomes. Qualitative context flags 'Driving / commuting' as a joint top activity (4/20), which is a contextually atypical use case for an anxiety DESCENT protocol and may be mildly suppressing scores; the 5-minute modal settle time is the fastest in the AX subcategory.
H-AX-L2.sphere_plain: Cottage produces consistent, positive results — people tend to settle into it quickly (around 5 minutes), which is the fastest in the anxiety group. An unexpected number of people were listening while driving or commuting, which isn't the ideal context for a calming descent protocol, and may explain why scores are slightly more mixed than Meadow. Worth flagging to the routing guidance.

H-AX-L3.name: Valley
H-AX-L3.sphere: H-AX-L3 (Valley, n=38, mean=6.97, std=1.81, meaningful confidence) performs near parity with L1 at the mean level, with qualitative data (n=20) showing 'Near the end / entire session' as a notable settle-time response (4/20) — suggesting a subset of listeners require the full protocol duration to entrain, consistent with the phi-sigmoid curve's more gradual gradient at L3. Rhythm is rated 'Very settling / calming' at its highest rate in the AX subcategory (10/20), indicating the L3 architecture is landing as designed for those who do settle.
H-AX-L3.sphere_plain: Valley holds up well as the longest anxiety protocol, and the people who do settle into it tend to find it deeply calming — the highest 'very settling' rhythm rating in the anxiety group. A meaningful subset needs the full session to get there, which is fine and expected for a longer format. Results are close to the L1 benchmark, which is a good sign for the extended architecture.

H-CR-L1.name: Anchor
H-CR-L1.sphere: H-CR-L1 (Anchor, n=48, mean=6.35, std=1.71, meaningful confidence) is the highest-scoring Crisis protocol, which is structurally expected as L1 listeners arrive in the most acute distress and have the most headroom for improvement. Qualitative context (n=24) is notably positive relative to the mean: 8/24 reported 'Significant improvement' and music opinion skews toward 'Liked it' / 'Loved it' — suggesting the protocol is achieving meaningful subjective relief even when numeric scores reflect constrained scale use by distressed listeners.
H-CR-L1.sphere_plain: Anchor is doing what a crisis protocol should: people arrive in acute distress and a meaningful number report significant improvement. Scores look modest on paper, but the 'significant improvement' reports in the qualitative data suggest real relief is happening — the numerical scale just has less room to move when someone starts from a very distressed place. Music and rhythm are well-received, which matters when someone is in crisis.

H-CR-L2.name: Harbor
H-CR-L2.sphere: H-CR-L2 (Harbor, n=35, mean=6.14, std=1.67, meaningful confidence) is the lowest-scoring protocol in the HEALING series outside of structural expectation. Qualitative data (n=16) shows 'Neutral / so-so / meh' as the leading music opinion (6/16) — unusual for a Crisis protocol — and 'Trying to sleep' appears as a top activity (3/16), suggesting some Adviser routing mismatch between sleep and crisis intent. This is the primary music-fit concern in the dataset.
H-CR-L2.sphere_plain: Harbor is the one protocol in the Healing series that feels like it could be working better. The music ratings are the weakest of any crisis protocol — a notable share found it unremarkable or underwhelming — and some people seem to be using it as a sleep aid rather than a crisis tool, which suggests the routing may need attention. It's not failing, but it's the clearest candidate for a music review in this series.

H-CR-L3.name: Horizon
H-CR-L3.sphere: H-CR-L3 (Horizon, n=29, mean=6.31, std=1.85, meaningful confidence) recovers modestly from L2 and shows the fastest settle-time signal in the Crisis subcategory: 7/19 contextual respondents reported settling 'Immediately / within the first minute or two.' However, music opinion includes the highest 'Not liked' frequency in the HEALING series (4/19), which warrants attention — a non-trivial minority are rejecting the sonic texture outright, possibly reflecting taste heterogeneity in a population under extreme stress.
H-CR-L3.sphere_plain: Horizon is interesting: a surprisingly large number of people say they settled almost immediately — within the first minute or two — which is unusually fast for a crisis protocol. On the other hand, a small but notable group said they actively didn't like the music, which is worth keeping an eye on. Overall results are in line with the other crisis protocols, with real improvement being reported.

H-SL-L1.name: Lantern
H-SL-L1.sphere: H-SL-L1 (Lantern, n=61, mean=6.97, std=1.89, strong confidence) anchors the Sleep subcategory with a strong mean and the widest std in the SL group, reflecting listener diversity. Qualitative data (n=15) shows a notably pure self-listener population (13/15) and strong music ratings ('Liked it' / 'Loved it' = 13/15), suggesting high aesthetic fit for direct listeners; the therapy session activity flag (3/15) is contextually unusual for a sleep protocol.
H-SL-L1.sphere_plain: Lantern is well-liked — almost everyone in the qualitative data who responded said they liked or loved the music, which is the strongest music reception in the Sleep group. Results are positive and consistent with a broad range of direct listeners using it for themselves. Some therapists are also using it in sessions, which is an unusual context for a sleep protocol but doesn't appear to be hurting outcomes.

H-SL-L2.name: Hammock
H-SL-L2.sphere: H-SL-L2 (Hammock, n=47, mean=7.04, std=1.46, meaningful confidence) has the tightest std in the HEALING series, indicating the most consistent outcomes across this subcategory. Qualitative data (n=15) shows a clinician/caregiver-dominant listener type (11/15 non-self), and 'Too busy' rhythm opinion appears at 3/15 — the only appearance of this rating in H-SL — which may reflect proxy listeners finding the rhythmic complexity distracting while supporting dependents toward sleep.
H-SL-L2.sphere_plain: Hammock produces the most consistent results of any protocol in the Healing series — very little variation between people, which is a good sign for reliability. Most of the people reporting on it are using it with or for someone else (caregivers, clinicians), and a few found the rhythm slightly busy in that context. That 'slightly busy' signal is worth investigating — it may not matter much for direct listeners falling asleep, but could be distracting for someone supporting another person.

H-SL-L3.name: Midnight
H-SL-L3.sphere: H-SL-L3 (Midnight, n=33, mean=7.03, std=1.57, meaningful confidence) maintains a high mean consistent with L2, and qualitative data (n=22) shows 'Slightly busy' as the second-ranked rhythm opinion (7/22) — the highest such rating in the SL subcategory. For a sleep-oriented DESCENT protocol at L3, 'slightly busy' rhythm perception in nearly a third of contextual respondents is worth monitoring; it may reflect the phi-sigmoid complexity at L3 interacting with sleep-onset sensitivity.
H-SL-L3.sphere_plain: Midnight performs similarly to Hammock and scores well overall. A notable share of listeners found the rhythm 'slightly busy', which is something to watch in a sleep protocol where any sense of complexity could disrupt wind-down. It's not a dominant complaint, but it's more present here than in L1 or L2. Results remain solidly positive.

T-FC-L1.name: Compass
T-FC-L1.sphere: T-FC-L1 (Compass, n=42, mean=6.40, std=1.77, meaningful confidence) is the lowest-mean protocol in the Focus & Clarity subcategory but qualitative data (n=23) tells a more positive story: 'Noticeable improvement' (10/23) and 'Significant improvement' (6/23) dominate change ratings. The most distinctive qualitative signal is 'Slightly busy' as the top rhythm opinion (9/23) — the strongest busy-rhythm signal in the THRIVING series — suggesting the L1 rhythmic density may be exceeding the tolerance of some listeners seeking focus.
T-FC-L1.sphere_plain: Compass gets positive reviews from listeners when they describe their experience in words — many report noticeable or significant improvement. But the rhythm stands out: more people described it as 'slightly busy' than any other protocol in the Thriving series. For a protocol designed to support focus and clarity, a busy-feeling rhythm might be working against some listeners. This is the clearest rhythm adjustment signal in the series.

T-FC-L2.name: Waypoint
T-FC-L2.sphere: T-FC-L2 (Waypoint, n=32, mean=7.09, std=1.51, meaningful confidence) is the strongest performer in the Focus & Clarity subcategory by mean, with a well-formed right-skewed distribution and a 10-minute modal settle time (9/17 contextual respondents). 'Slightly busy' rhythm opinion appears at 5/17 — attenuated from L1 but still present — and 'Care-giving / supporting someone else' is the top activity (5/17), indicating significant proxy listener presence at L2.
T-FC-L2.sphere_plain: Waypoint is the strongest focus protocol by outcome score and produces good results across a range of listeners. It takes about 10 minutes to settle into, which is typical for this type of protocol. A noticeable number of listeners are using it while supporting someone else, rather than for themselves — which is an interesting use pattern worth understanding better.

T-FC-L3.name: Summit
T-FC-L3.sphere: T-FC-L3 (Summit, n=22, mean=6.23, std=1.38, meaningful confidence) represents the sharpest within-subcategory mean inversion in the dataset: L3 scores 0.86 below L2. Qualitative data (n=26) paradoxically shows 'Significant improvement' as the top change rating (11/26) — a marked divergence between the 1-second outcome score and 1-minute qualitative improvement rating that warrants methodological investigation. Modal settle time is 10 minutes (14/26), and rhythm is predominantly 'Balanced / comfortable', so the protocol is not being experienced as aversive; the score suppression mechanism is unclear.
T-FC-L3.sphere_plain: Summit is the most puzzling protocol in the Thriving series. Outcome scores are the lowest in the Focus group, but when people describe how they felt, the most common response is 'significant improvement' — a genuine mismatch between the two measures. It's not aversive; the rhythm is comfortable and people take a reasonable amount of time to settle. Something about how the experience translates to a number on the scale seems to be breaking down here, and it's worth investigating.

T-CF-L1.name: Ember
T-CF-L1.sphere: T-CF-L1 (Ember, n=38, mean=6.66, std=1.63, meaningful confidence) opens the Creative Flow subcategory with a moderate positive signal. Qualitative data (n=21) shows 'Slight improvement' as the top change rating (8/21), which is lower than typical for THRIVING — suggesting listeners may be entering with higher baseline states and reporting smaller delta. Rhythm is rated 'Very settling / calming' at 6/21, and immediate settle time is joint-modal (6/21), indicating rapid entrainment for a segment of this population.
T-CF-L1.sphere_plain: Ember gets people into a creative or focused state reasonably well, but the most common description of change is 'slight improvement' — which may reflect that people starting this protocol are already in a reasonably good place and don't have as far to travel. Many people settle into it quickly, within the first couple of minutes. A functional foundation, but the improvement reports are more modest than in some other Thriving protocols.

T-CF-L2.name: Current
T-CF-L2.sphere: T-CF-L2 (Current, n=24, mean=6.58, std=1.28, meaningful confidence) shows the tightest std in the CF subcategory and the highest 'Loved it!' music opinion rate so far in THRIVING (6/15 contextual), suggesting strong aesthetic resonance with a more experienced listener cohort. The mean dips slightly from L1 (-0.08), but given the reduced variance, the consistency signal is arguably stronger. 'Driving / commuting' appears as a top activity (3/15), which is atypical for a creative flow protocol.
T-CF-L2.sphere_plain: Current is the most consistent creative flow protocol — very little variation between listeners, and a strong share said they loved the music outright. It works steadily if not dramatically, and the tight results suggest it suits a fairly reliable listener profile. A small number of people listening while driving is an unusual use case worth noting.

T-CF-L3.name: Aurora
T-CF-L3.sphere: T-CF-L3 (Aurora, n=18, mean=7.17, std=1.15, developing confidence) is the highest-mean protocol in the Creative Flow subcategory and shows the tightest std across all CF levels, though n=18 limits interpretive confidence. Qualitative data (n=25) reveals a high caregiver/clinician proxy presence (15/25 non-self) and 'Trying to sleep' as a top activity (5/25) — suggesting Aurora is being used cross-purpose. Despite this, change ratings are positive and rhythm is predominantly 'Balanced / comfortable', indicating the HOLD architecture is functioning well for this heterogeneous listener group.
T-CF-L3.sphere_plain: Aurora is the highest-rated creative flow protocol and the results are impressively consistent — though only 18 people have scored it so far, so we're not ready to draw firm conclusions. A large proportion of listeners are proxies (caregivers, clinicians) rather than direct listeners, and some are using it for sleep rather than creative flow. Despite this, it's performing well. Worth watching closely as the sample grows.

T-MB-L1.name: Stone
T-MB-L1.sphere: T-MB-L1 (Stone, n=29, mean=7.17, std=1.31, meaningful confidence) is the highest-mean protocol in the Maintenance & Balance subcategory and among the most consistent in THRIVING. Qualitative data (n=23) shows 'Therapy session' as the dominant activity (8/23) — the highest clinical context rate in the series — and a 20-minute modal settle time (9/23), consistent with a deliberate, extended HOLD architecture in a formal therapeutic setting. Music opinion is mixed (neutral and liked roughly equal), suggesting aesthetic neutrality is acceptable in this structured context.
T-MB-L1.sphere_plain: Stone is a strong, reliable maintenance protocol — highest-scoring in its group with very consistent results. It's heavily used in therapy sessions, more than almost any other Thriving protocol, and the longer settle time (around 20 minutes) fits that structured clinical context well. Music opinion is mixed rather than warm, which is fine — in a formal therapeutic setting, aesthetic neutrality isn't a problem.

T-MB-L2.name: Axis
T-MB-L2.sphere: T-MB-L2 (Axis, n=22, mean=6.59, std=1.97, meaningful confidence) has the highest std in the THRIVING series, indicating meaningful outcome heterogeneity. Qualitative data (n=16) shows an even listener-type split (7 self, 7 client/patient) and 'Slightly busy' rhythm opinion at 7/16 — joint-highest in THRIVING — suggesting the L2 rhythmic complexity may be landing differently across listener types. The distribution shows a wide spread from 1 to 10, consistent with the subgroup-effect hypothesis.
T-MB-L2.sphere_plain: Axis shows the most variation in outcomes of any Thriving protocol, meaning some people get a lot from it and others don't respond much. The qualitative data splits almost evenly between direct listeners and clients/patients, which may partly explain that spread. The rhythm is described as 'slightly busy' more often than any other maintenance protocol — something that could be pulling results apart depending on the listener's state and context.

T-MB-L3.name: Orbit
T-MB-L3.sphere: T-MB-L3 (Orbit, n=15, mean=6.87, std=1.64, developing confidence) has the highest pct_pos in the dataset at 73%, yet qualitative data (n=19) shows 4/19 respondents reporting 'Did NOT settle' — the highest non-settling rate in THRIVING and an unusual signal for a HOLD protocol. These two signals are not necessarily contradictory (those who do settle may score very high, pulling the mean up), but the distribution spike at score=7 (9/15) and the non-settling reports together suggest a binary response pattern that warrants investigation at higher n.
T-MB-L3.sphere_plain: Orbit has the highest share of positive outcomes in the entire Thriving series, yet a meaningful number of qualitative respondents say they never settled into it at all. Those two facts can coexist — the people who do connect with it seem to find it genuinely helpful, while others aren't getting there at all. That non-settling signal is unusual for a HOLD protocol and suggests the entry into the hold phase may need adjustment.

X-ME-L1.name: Breeze
X-ME-L1.sphere: X-ME-L1 (Breeze, n=35, mean=6.29, std=1.71, meaningful confidence) opens Mood & Energy with a moderate signal and the joint-lowest pct_pos in TRANSFORMING at 43%. Qualitative data (n=22) is meaningfully positive: 'Loved it!' leads music opinion (10/22) and change ratings are distributed across noticeable, significant, and moderate improvement categories. The 20-minute modal settle time (6/22) is the longest in ME and consistent with an ASCENT protocol requiring extended entrainment for a low-affect population.
X-ME-L1.sphere_plain: Breeze is the gentlest entry into the Mood and Energy group and produces modest but real results. People love the music — the strongest music enthusiasm in the Transforming series — and the improvement descriptions in qualitative responses are genuinely positive. It takes a while to settle (around 20 minutes), which makes sense for a protocol gently lifting people from a low starting point. Patience is built into how it works.

X-ME-L2.name: Sunrise
X-ME-L2.sphere: X-ME-L2 (Sunrise, n=25, mean=6.48, std=1.64, meaningful confidence) improves modestly on L1 with pct_pos rising to 60%. Qualitative data (n=15) shows 'Significant improvement' as the top change rating (7/15) — the most positive qualitative signal in the ME subcategory — and music opinion is warm ('Liked it' + 'Loved it!' = 13/15). Settle time shows a bimodal split (immediate vs. 20 minutes), which is consistent with either a fast-responder / slow-responder subgroup dynamic or varied listener entry states.
X-ME-L2.sphere_plain: Sunrise shows the strongest positive improvement descriptions of the Mood and Energy group, with 'significant improvement' leading the qualitative responses. Music is well-liked. Settle time splits interestingly between very fast (immediate) and very slow (20 minutes), suggesting there may be two distinct types of listeners using this protocol — one group that clicks with it immediately and one that needs more time, or starts from a lower place.

X-ME-L3.name: Updraft
X-ME-L3.sphere: X-ME-L3 (Updraft, n=18, mean=6.28, std=1.74, developing confidence) is the most contextually well-evidenced protocol in the ME subcategory by qualitative n (25 contextual responses vs. 18 outcome scores, suggesting high 1-minute survey engagement). 'No effect' appears in 5/25 change ratings — the highest non-response rate in ME — while music opinion is positive ('Liked it' + 'Loved it!' = 18/25). Rhythm is split evenly between 'Very settling / calming' and 'Balanced / comfortable', suggesting the L3 ASCENT architecture is sonically appropriate but not achieving consistent functional lift for all listeners.
X-ME-L3.sphere_plain: Updraft is generating a lot of listener feedback relative to how many people have scored it, which suggests engaged listeners. The music is well-received, and the rhythm feels appropriate. The main concern is that about a fifth of qualitative respondents report no effect — higher than expected for this group. It may be that some listeners reaching L3 are in states too low for the protocol to lift meaningfully within its current timeframe.

X-MD-L1.name: Footpath
X-MD-L1.sphere: X-MD-L1 (Footpath, n=21, mean=6.24, std=1.97, meaningful confidence) anchors the Motivation & Drive ASCENT subcategory with the lowest pct_pos in TRANSFORMING at 43%. Qualitative data (n=17) is more positive: 'Noticeable improvement' (6/17) and 'Significant improvement' (5/17) lead change ratings, and settle time is fast (modal ~5 minutes, with 4/17 settling immediately). The divergence between the numeric score and qualitative improvement rating is consistent with the hypothesis that ASCENT protocol listeners in low-affect states compress their 0–10 scale usage.
X-MD-L1.sphere_plain: Footpath is the entry point for the Motivation and Drive group, targeting listeners in genuinely low-affect states. Outcome scores are modest, but qualitative reports are more positive — many describe noticeable or significant improvement. People settle into it quickly, which is a good sign. The gap between how it scores and how people describe it is consistent with what we'd expect when someone starts from a very low place and finds it harder to give a high number even when things improve.

X-MD-L2.name: Trail
X-MD-L2.sphere: X-MD-L2 (Trail, n=14, mean=6.0, std=1.57, developing confidence) is the lowest-mean protocol in the Motivation & Drive subcategory and shows pct_pos at 36% — the lowest in the dataset. Qualitative data (n=21) is more encouraging: 'Loved it!' leads music opinion (9/21) and rhythm is 'Balanced / comfortable' (9/21), suggesting the listening experience is well-received. The 20-minute modal settle time (5/21) is the longest in the MD subcategory and may indicate that Trail's entrainment gradient requires more time than the current protocol length accommodates.
X-MD-L2.sphere_plain: Trail has the lowest positive-outcome rate in the dataset, which is notable. Yet listeners say they love the music — a strong endorsement — and the rhythm feels comfortable. The main issue seems to be time: the modal settle time is 20 minutes, the longest in this group, suggesting it may take longer than the current protocol allows to actually lift people who are in a low-motivation state. An extended version might perform better.

X-MD-L3.name: Switchback
X-MD-L3.sphere: X-MD-L3 (Switchback, n=12, mean=6.92, std=2.31, developing confidence) is the highest-mean protocol in the MD subcategory but also the most variable, with a distribution suggesting a bimodal-adjacent split between low (3–4) and high (8–9) scorers. Qualitative data (n=17) shows 'Trying to sleep' as the top activity (6/17), which is notably misaligned with an ASCENT motivation protocol — this cross-purpose usage is a plausible driver of the bimodal pattern and should be investigated via Adviser routing review.
X-MD-L3.sphere_plain: Switchback scores well on average, but the results are all over the place — some listeners score it very low, others very high, with less in between. A striking finding is that many people are using it to try to sleep, even though it's designed to lift energy and motivation. Those two use cases don't really fit together, and this mismatch is the most likely reason for the split results. Routing guidance may need to clarify when Switchback is and isn't appropriate.

X-MC-L1.name: Cavern
X-MC-L1.sphere: X-MC-L1 (Cavern, n=24, mean=6.62, std=1.56, meaningful confidence) opens TRANSFORMING's Mind & Clarity subcategory with a moderate positive signal. Qualitative data (n=25) is notably warm: 'Loved it!' and 'Liked it' together account for 18/25 music opinions — the strongest music reception in the TRANSFORMING series — and rhythm is split between 'Balanced / comfortable' and 'Very settling / calming'. Two respondents reported 'It actually made things worse', which is unusual and worth tracking as n grows.
X-MC-L1.sphere_plain: Cavern is the most musically well-received protocol in the Transforming series — listeners say they liked or loved it more than almost anything else in this series. Outcomes are moderately positive, and people describe settling into it steadily, typically within 5–10 minutes. Two listeners reported feeling worse after using it, which is a rare signal across the whole dataset and worth keeping an eye on.

X-MC-L2.name: Starlight
X-MC-L2.sphere: X-MC-L2 (Starlight, n=17, mean=6.59, std=1.37, developing confidence) shows a tight, moderately positive distribution. Qualitative data (n=20) skews heavily self-listener (16/20) and 'Very settling / calming' dominates rhythm opinion (12/20) — the strongest settling rhythm perception in the TRANSFORMING series. 'No effect' appears in 3/20 change ratings, a notable non-responder signal for a small qualitative sample.
X-MC-L2.sphere_plain: Starlight produces calm, consistent results — tight outcome spread and the strongest 'very settling' rhythm perception in the Transforming series. Most listeners are using it for themselves, and a small number reported no effect, which is worth noting for a protocol aimed at supporting mental clarity. Overall, a quietly solid protocol.

X-MC-L3.name: Cosmos
X-MC-L3.sphere: X-MC-L3 (Cosmos, n=10, mean=5.6, std=2.32, developing confidence) is the lowest-scoring protocol in the dataset by mean and the highest in variance. The distribution is effectively flat from scores 2–9, which at n=10 most likely reflects sampling noise rather than a genuine uniform response pattern. Qualitative data (n=16) shows 7/16 are client/patient-proxy listeners — the highest clinical proxy rate in TRANSFORMING — and 'Noticeable improvement' dominates change ratings (9/16), creating a similar qualitative-quantitative divergence to T-FC-L3. No conclusions should be drawn until n≥30.
X-MC-L3.sphere_plain: Cosmos has only been heard by 10 people so far, which means the results — including a below-average mean score — should be treated as early noise rather than a meaningful signal. A large proportion of responses come from clinicians or caregivers rather than direct listeners, and those who described their experience in words reported mostly positive improvement. It's simply too early to say much. Check back when there are at least 30 outcome scores.

sphere_commentary.overview: Across 1,000 outcome scores spanning 30 protocols, the aggregate signal is moderately positive and consistent with a therapeutic music intervention in real-world use: series means cluster between 6.36 and 6.78, standard deviations hover around 1.6–1.9, and positive-outcome rates (scores ≥6) range from 43% to 73%, indicating meaningful but unevenly distributed benefit. No series is underperforming in a way that warrants structural concern at this stage; variance is better read as population diversity than as protocol instability. The 607 contextual responses add interpretive texture — listener-type heterogeneity (direct listeners, caregivers, clinicians) and varied use contexts (driving, therapy sessions, sleep attempts) are plausibly contributing to score spread across all series.
sphere_commentary.cross_series: HEALING leads on both mean (6.78) and sample size (n=582), with its tightest clusters in the Sleep and Anxiety subcategories, suggesting these use cases attract a relatively homogeneous, well-matched population; the Crisis subcategory's lower means are structurally expected given the extreme entry states of that listener group. THRIVING (mean=6.73, n=242) is performing comparably to HEALING despite being designed for maintenance and optimisation rather than acute relief — a notable finding, since optimisation-goal listeners typically have less headroom to report improvement on a 0–10 scale. TRANSFORMING (mean=6.36, n=176) scores lowest across all three series, which is consistent with its ascending-arc protocol design and the clinical difficulty of the low-affect populations it targets; however, its ASCENT-type protocols show elevated variance and some distributional irregularity that warrants closer inspection as sample sizes grow.
sphere_commentary.anomalies: X-MC-L3 (Cosmos, n=10) is the most structurally anomalous protocol in the dataset: mean=5.6 with std=2.32 and a distribution that is notably flat across the 3–9 range, suggesting either population mismatch, ceiling/floor effects, or early noise given the very small n — no conclusions should be drawn, but it is the clearest flag for monitoring. X-MD-L3 (Switchback, n=12) shows std=2.31 with a bimodal-adjacent pattern (scores clustering at both 3–4 and 8–9), consistent with a subgroup split — possibly listeners in genuinely different motivational states responding to an ASCENT protocol very differently. T-MB-L3 (Orbit, n=15) shows a striking distributional spike: 9 of 15 respondents scored exactly 7, with zeros at 6 and 8 — this is almost certainly a small-n artefact but the quantitative data also shows 4 of 19 contextual respondents reported 'Did NOT settle', an unusually high non-settling rate for a HOLD protocol. T-FC-L3 (Summit) has the lowest pct_pos in THRIVING at 45% (mean=6.23) despite contextual responders reporting predominantly 'Significant improvement' in change_rating — this divergence between the 1-second score and the 1-minute qualitative rating is a methodologically interesting signal that may reflect response-time effects or outcome framing differences. H-CR-L2 (Harbor) music_opinion shows the most neutral/negative lean in the HEALING series ('Neutral / so-so / meh...' leading negatively at n=6), which may be suppressing outcome scores independently of the protocol's therapeutic efficacy.
sphere_commentary.development_signals:
1. X-MC-L3 (Cosmos, n=10, mean=5.6, std=2.32): The combination of very low n, high variance, and the flattest distribution in the dataset makes this protocol the highest-priority monitoring candidate in TRANSFORMING. Recommended action: hold design iteration until n reaches at least 30, then examine whether the distribution resolves toward a normal curve or confirms a bimodal subgroup split. If bimodal, investigate whether listener-type (clinician-proxy vs. self-report) is the segmentation variable — qualitative data shows 7 of 16 contextual respondents were clients/patients, the highest clinical-proxy ratio in X-MC.

2. T-FC-L3 (Summit, n=22, mean=6.23, pct_pos=45%) vs. T-FC-L2 (Waypoint, n=32, mean=7.09, pct_pos=66%): The L2-to-L3 performance inversion in the Focus & Clarity subcategory is the sharpest within-subcategory drop in the dataset. Contextual data for T-FC-L3 shows 'Slightly busy' as the second-ranked rhythm opinion (6/26) and a 10-minute modal settle time — suggesting the L3 protocol's complexity or length may be exceeding the entrainment tolerance of this population. Recommended action: review the WING RATIO implementation in Summit specifically, and consider whether the phi-sigmoid curve is advancing too steeply for a HOLD protocol at this level. A rhythm-simplified L3 variant is worth prototyping.

3. H-CR-L2 (Harbor, n=35, mean=6.14): Music opinion is the weakest in the HEALING series — 'Neutral / so-so / meh' leads or co-leads in the 1-minute data, and pct_pos sits at 46%. Given that H-CR-L1 (Anchor) and H-CR-L3 (Horizon) both score higher on music opinion and outcome, Harbor appears to be the weak link in the Crisis subcategory's routing chain. Recommended action: audit Harbor's timbral and harmonic profile relative to Anchor and Horizon; the data is consistent with a music-fit mismatch rather than a structural protocol problem. Consider A/B testing an alternative composition within the same DESCENT architecture.

4. T-MB-L3 (Orbit, n=15, developing confidence): Four of 19 contextual respondents reported 'Did NOT settle' — the highest non-settling signal in the THRIVING series. For a HOLD protocol at L3, non-settling is a meaningful misfire indicator. The protocol also shows a quantitatively unusual distribution spike at score=7. Recommended action: increase the hold phase duration and review the transition architecture into the hold phase; if listeners are not settling within the protocol window, the onset may be advancing too quickly for L3 Maintenance & Balance use cases. Flag for re-review at n=30.

by_protocol.H-AX-L1: H-AX-L1 (Meadow, n=65, mean=7.14, std=1.89, strong confidence) is the highest-performing protocol in the HEALING series by mean score, with pct_pos at 62% and a right-skewed distribution peaking at scores 6–9. Qualitative context (n=23) shows a substantial caregiver/clinician proxy presence (10/23) and a modal settle time of ~10 minutes, with rhythm predominantly rated 'Balanced / comfortable' — consistent with a well-calibrated DESCENT onset for a general anxiety population.
by_protocol.H-AX-L2: H-AX-L2 (Cottage, n=51, mean=6.90, std=1.63, strong confidence) shows a tighter distribution than L1 with slightly lower std, suggesting more consistent — if marginally lower ceiling — outcomes. Qualitative context flags 'Driving / commuting' as a joint top activity (4/20), which is a contextually atypical use case for an anxiety DESCENT protocol and may be mildly suppressing scores; the 5-minute modal settle time is the fastest in the AX subcategory.
by_protocol.H-AX-L3: H-AX-L3 (Valley, n=38, mean=6.97, std=1.81, meaningful confidence) performs near parity with L1 at the mean level, with qualitative data (n=20) showing 'Near the end / entire session' as a notable settle-time response (4/20) — suggesting a subset of listeners require the full protocol duration to entrain, consistent with the phi-sigmoid curve's more gradual gradient at L3. Rhythm is rated 'Very settling / calming' at its highest rate in the AX subcategory (10/20), indicating the L3 architecture is landing as designed for those who do settle.
by_protocol.H-CR-L1: H-CR-L1 (Anchor, n=48, mean=6.35, std=1.71, meaningful confidence) is the highest-scoring Crisis protocol, which is structurally expected as L1 listeners arrive in the most acute distress and have the most headroom for improvement. Qualitative context (n=24) is notably positive relative to the mean: 8/24 reported 'Significant improvement' and music opinion skews toward 'Liked it' / 'Loved it' — suggesting the protocol is achieving meaningful subjective relief even when numeric scores reflect constrained scale use by distressed listeners.
by_protocol.H-CR-L2: H-CR-L2 (Harbor, n=35, mean=6.14, std=1.67, meaningful confidence) is the lowest-scoring protocol in the HEALING series outside of structural expectation. Qualitative data (n=16) shows 'Neutral / so-so / meh' as the leading music opinion (6/16) — unusual for a Crisis protocol — and 'Trying to sleep' appears as a top activity (3/16), suggesting some Adviser routing mismatch between sleep and crisis intent. This is the primary music-fit concern in the dataset.
by_protocol.H-CR-L3: H-CR-L3 (Horizon, n=29, mean=6.31, std=1.85, meaningful confidence) recovers modestly from L2 and shows the fastest settle-time signal in the Crisis subcategory: 7/19 contextual respondents reported settling 'Immediately / within the first minute or two.' However, music opinion includes the highest 'Not liked' frequency in the HEALING series (4/19), which warrants attention — a non-trivial minority are rejecting the sonic texture outright, possibly reflecting taste heterogeneity in a population under extreme stress.
by_protocol.H-OS-L1: H-OS-L1 (Pebble, n=73, mean=6.55, std=1.80, strong confidence) is the largest single-protocol sample in the dataset and provides the most stable estimate in the Over-stimulation subcategory. Distribution is approximately normal with a modest right skew; qualitative data (n=27) shows 'Driving / commuting' and 'Just listening' as joint top activities, suggesting a heterogeneous use context that plausibly contributes to the moderate std. Rhythm ratings split evenly between 'Balanced / comfortable' and 'Very settling / calming', indicating the L1 architecture is landing across a broad listener range.
by_protocol.H-OS-L2: H-OS-L2 (Rainfall, n=58, mean=6.71, std=1.85, strong confidence) outperforms L1 by mean (+0.16), with qualitative data (n=20) showing 'Noticeable improvement' as the dominant change rating (10/20) and a 10-minute modal settle time — the longest in the OS subcategory. The higher settle time at L2 is consistent with the wing-ratio architecture extending entrainment duration at longer protocol lengths.
by_protocol.H-OS-L3: H-OS-L3 (Glacier, n=44, mean=6.98, std=1.78, meaningful confidence) is the strongest performer in the OS subcategory by mean, with qualitative data (n=23) revealing 'Trying to sleep' as the top activity (8/23) — the highest sleep-use rate for a non-Sleep protocol. This cross-purpose usage is worth flagging: listeners may be self-routing to Glacier for sleep rather than over-stimulation recovery, which could be inflating or deflating scores depending on how well the DESCENT architecture serves sleep onset.
by_protocol.H-SL-L1: H-SL-L1 (Lantern, n=61, mean=6.97, std=1.89, strong confidence) anchors the Sleep subcategory with a strong mean and the widest std in the SL group, reflecting listener diversity. Qualitative data (n=15) shows a notably pure self-listener population (13/15) and strong music ratings ('Liked it' / 'Loved it' = 13/15), suggesting high aesthetic fit for direct listeners; the therapy session activity flag (3/15) is contextually unusual for a sleep protocol.
by_protocol.H-SL-L2: H-SL-L2 (Hammock, n=47, mean=7.04, std=1.46, meaningful confidence) has the tightest std in the HEALING series, indicating the most consistent outcomes across this subcategory. Qualitative data (n=15) shows a clinician/caregiver-dominant listener type (11/15 non-self), and 'Too busy' rhythm opinion appears at 3/15 — the only appearance of this rating in H-SL — which may reflect proxy listeners finding the rhythmic complexity distracting while supporting dependents toward sleep.
by_protocol.H-SL-L3: H-SL-L3 (Midnight, n=33, mean=7.03, std=1.57, meaningful confidence) maintains a high mean consistent with L2, and qualitative data (n=22) shows 'Slightly busy' as the second-ranked rhythm opinion (7/22) — the highest such rating in the SL subcategory. For a sleep-oriented DESCENT protocol at L3, 'slightly busy' rhythm perception in nearly a third of contextual respondents is worth monitoring; it may reflect the phi-sigmoid complexity at L3 interacting with sleep-onset sensitivity.
by_protocol.T-CF-L1: T-CF-L1 (Ember, n=38, mean=6.66, std=1.63, meaningful confidence) opens the Creative Flow subcategory with a moderate positive signal. Qualitative data (n=21) shows 'Slight improvement' as the top change rating (8/21), which is lower than typical for THRIVING — suggesting listeners may be entering with higher baseline states and reporting smaller delta. Rhythm is rated 'Very settling / calming' at 6/21, and immediate settle time is joint-modal (6/21), indicating rapid entrainment for a segment of this population.
by_protocol.T-CF-L2: T-CF-L2 (Current, n=24, mean=6.58, std=1.28, meaningful confidence) shows the tightest std in the CF subcategory and the highest 'Loved it!' music opinion rate so far in THRIVING (6/15 contextual), suggesting strong aesthetic resonance with a more experienced listener cohort. The mean dips slightly from L1 (-0.08), but given the reduced variance, the consistency signal is arguably stronger. 'Driving / commuting' appears as a top activity (3/15), which is atypical for a creative flow protocol.
by_protocol.T-CF-L3: T-CF-L3 (Aurora, n=18, mean=7.17, std=1.15, developing confidence) is the highest-mean protocol in the Creative Flow subcategory and shows the tightest std across all CF levels, though n=18 limits interpretive confidence. Qualitative data (n=25) reveals a high caregiver/clinician proxy presence (15/25 non-self) and 'Trying to sleep' as a top activity (5/25) — suggesting Aurora is being used cross-purpose. Despite this, change ratings are positive and rhythm is predominantly 'Balanced / comfortable', indicating the HOLD architecture is functioning well for this heterogeneous listener group.
by_protocol.T-FC-L1: T-FC-L1 (Compass, n=42, mean=6.40, std=1.77, meaningful confidence) is the lowest-mean protocol in the Focus & Clarity subcategory but qualitative data (n=23) tells a more positive story: 'Noticeable improvement' (10/23) and 'Significant improvement' (6/23) dominate change ratings. The most distinctive qualitative signal is 'Slightly busy' as the top rhythm opinion (9/23) — the strongest busy-rhythm signal in the THRIVING series — suggesting the L1 rhythmic density may be exceeding the tolerance of some listeners seeking focus.
by_protocol.T-FC-L2: T-FC-L2 (Waypoint, n=32, mean=7.09, std=1.51, meaningful confidence) is the strongest performer in the Focus & Clarity subcategory by mean, with a well-formed right-skewed distribution and a 10-minute modal settle time (9/17 contextual respondents). 'Slightly busy' rhythm opinion appears at 5/17 — attenuated from L1 but still present — and 'Care-giving / supporting someone else' is the top activity (5/17), indicating significant proxy listener presence at L2.
by_protocol.T-FC-L3: T-FC-L3 (Summit, n=22, mean=6.23, std=1.38, meaningful confidence) represents the sharpest within-subcategory mean inversion in the dataset: L3 scores 0.86 below L2. Qualitative data (n=26) paradoxically shows 'Significant improvement' as the top change rating (11/26) — a marked divergence between the 1-second outcome score and 1-minute qualitative improvement rating that warrants methodological investigation. Modal settle time is 10 minutes (14/26), and rhythm is predominantly 'Balanced / comfortable', so the protocol is not being experienced as aversive; the score suppression mechanism is unclear.
by_protocol.T-MB-L1: T-MB-L1 (Stone, n=29, mean=7.17, std=1.31, meaningful confidence) is the highest-mean protocol in the Maintenance & Balance subcategory and among the most consistent in THRIVING. Qualitative data (n=23) shows 'Therapy session' as the dominant activity (8/23) — the highest clinical context rate in the series — and a 20-minute modal settle time (9/23), consistent with a deliberate, extended HOLD architecture in a formal therapeutic setting. Music opinion is mixed (neutral and liked roughly equal), suggesting aesthetic neutrality is acceptable in this structured context.
by_protocol.T-MB-L2: T-MB-L2 (Axis, n=22, mean=6.59, std=1.97, meaningful confidence) has the highest std in the THRIVING series, indicating meaningful outcome heterogeneity. Qualitative data (n=16) shows an even listener-type split (7 self, 7 client/patient) and 'Slightly busy' rhythm opinion at 7/16 — joint-highest in THRIVING — suggesting the L2 rhythmic complexity may be landing differently across listener types. The distribution shows a wide spread from 1 to 10, consistent with the subgroup-effect hypothesis.
by_protocol.T-MB-L3: T-MB-L3 (Orbit, n=15, mean=6.87, std=1.64, developing confidence) has the highest pct_pos in the dataset at 73%, yet qualitative data (n=19) shows 4/19 respondents reporting 'Did NOT settle' — the highest non-settling rate in THRIVING and an unusual signal for a HOLD protocol. These two signals are not necessarily contradictory (those who do settle may score very high, pulling the mean up), but the distribution spike at score=7 (9/15) and the non-settling reports together suggest a binary response pattern that warrants investigation at higher n.
by_protocol.X-MC-L1: X-MC-L1 (Cavern, n=24, mean=6.62, std=1.56, meaningful confidence) opens TRANSFORMING's Mind & Clarity subcategory with a moderate positive signal. Qualitative data (n=25) is notably warm: 'Loved it!' and 'Liked it' together account for 18/25 music opinions — the strongest music reception in the TRANSFORMING series — and rhythm is split between 'Balanced / comfortable' and 'Very settling / calming'. Two respondents reported 'It actually made things worse', which is unusual and worth tracking as n grows.
by_protocol.X-MC-L2: X-MC-L2 (Starlight, n=17, mean=6.59, std=1.37, developing confidence) shows a tight, moderately positive distribution. Qualitative data (n=20) skews heavily self-listener (16/20) and 'Very settling / calming' dominates rhythm opinion (12/20) — the strongest settling rhythm perception in the TRANSFORMING series. 'No effect' appears in 3/20 change ratings, a notable non-responder signal for a small qualitative sample.
by_protocol.X-MC-L3: X-MC-L3 (Cosmos, n=10, mean=5.6, std=2.32, developing confidence) is the lowest-scoring protocol in the dataset by mean and the highest in variance. The distribution is effectively flat from scores 2–9, which at n=10 most likely reflects sampling noise rather than a genuine uniform response pattern. Qualitative data (n=16) shows 7/16 are client/patient-proxy listeners — the highest clinical proxy rate in TRANSFORMING — and 'Noticeable improvement' dominates change ratings (9/16), creating a similar qualitative-quantitative divergence to T-FC-L3. No conclusions should be drawn until n≥30.
by_protocol.X-MD-L1: X-MD-L1 (Footpath, n=21, mean=6.24, std=1.97, meaningful confidence) anchors the Motivation & Drive ASCENT subcategory with the lowest pct_pos in TRANSFORMING at 43%. Qualitative data (n=17) is more positive: 'Noticeable improvement' (6/17) and 'Significant improvement' (5/17) lead change ratings, and settle time is fast (modal ~5 minutes, with 4/17 settling immediately). The divergence between the numeric score and qualitative improvement rating is consistent with the hypothesis that ASCENT protocol listeners in low-affect states compress their 0–10 scale usage.
by_protocol.X-MD-L2: X-MD-L2 (Trail, n=14, mean=6.0, std=1.57, developing confidence) is the lowest-mean protocol in the Motivation & Drive subcategory and shows pct_pos at 36% — the lowest in the dataset. Qualitative data (n=21) is more encouraging: 'Loved it!' leads music opinion (9/21) and rhythm is 'Balanced / comfortable' (9/21), suggesting the listening experience is well-received. The 20-minute modal settle time (5/21) is the longest in the MD subcategory and may indicate that Trail's entrainment gradient requires more time than the current protocol length accommodates.
by_protocol.X-MD-L3: X-MD-L3 (Switchback, n=12, mean=6.92, std=2.31, developing confidence) is the highest-mean protocol in the MD subcategory but also the most variable, with a distribution suggesting a bimodal-adjacent split between low (3–4) and high (8–9) scorers. Qualitative data (n=17) shows 'Trying to sleep' as the top activity (6/17), which is notably misaligned with an ASCENT motivation protocol — this cross-purpose usage is a plausible driver of the bimodal pattern and should be investigated via Adviser routing review.
by_protocol.X-ME-L1: X-ME-L1 (Breeze, n=35, mean=6.29, std=1.71, meaningful confidence) opens Mood & Energy with a moderate signal and the joint-lowest pct_pos in TRANSFORMING at 43%. Qualitative data (n=22) is meaningfully positive: 'Loved it!' leads music opinion (10/22) and change ratings are distributed across noticeable, significant, and moderate improvement categories. The 20-minute modal settle time (6/22) is the longest in ME and consistent with an ASCENT protocol requiring extended entrainment for a low-affect population.
by_protocol.X-ME-L2: X-ME-L2 (Sunrise, n=25, mean=6.48, std=1.64, meaningful confidence) improves modestly on L1 with pct_pos rising to 60%. Qualitative data (n=15) shows 'Significant improvement' as the top change rating (7/15) — the most positive qualitative signal in the ME subcategory — and music opinion is warm ('Liked it' + 'Loved it!' = 13/15). Settle time shows a bimodal split (immediate vs. 20 minutes), which is consistent with either a fast-responder / slow-responder subgroup dynamic or varied listener entry states.
by_protocol.X-ME-L3: X-ME-L3 (Updraft, n=18, mean=6.28, std=1.74, developing confidence) is the most contextually well-evidenced protocol in the ME subcategory by qualitative n (25 contextual responses vs. 18 outcome scores, suggesting high 1-minute survey engagement). 'No effect' appears in 5/25 change ratings — the highest non-response rate in ME — while music opinion is positive ('Liked it' + 'Loved it!' = 18/25). Rhythm is split evenly between 'Very settling / calming' and 'Balanced / comfortable', suggesting the L3 ASCENT architecture is sonically appropriate but not achieving consistent functional lift for all listeners.

by_protocol_plain.H-AX-L1: Meadow is the top performer in the Healing series — more people report feeling better from it than almost any other protocol, and the improvement tends to feel real and noticeable rather than marginal. People using it on behalf of someone else (caregivers, therapists) make up a significant chunk of responses, and most say it felt comfortable rather than overwhelming. A solid, reliable starting point for anxiety and stress.
by_protocol_plain.H-AX-L2: Cottage produces consistent, positive results — people tend to settle into it quickly (around 5 minutes), which is the fastest in the anxiety group. An unexpected number of people were listening while driving or commuting, which isn't the ideal context for a calming descent protocol, and may explain why scores are slightly more mixed than Meadow. Worth flagging to the routing guidance.
by_protocol_plain.H-AX-L3: Valley holds up well as the longest anxiety protocol, and the people who do settle into it tend to find it deeply calming — the highest 'very settling' rhythm rating in the anxiety group. A meaningful subset needs the full session to get there, which is fine and expected for a longer format. Results are close to the L1 benchmark, which is a good sign for the extended architecture.
by_protocol_plain.H-CR-L1: Anchor is doing what a crisis protocol should: people arrive in acute distress and a meaningful number report significant improvement. Scores look modest on paper, but the 'significant improvement' reports in the qualitative data suggest real relief is happening — the numerical scale just has less room to move when someone starts from a very distressed place. Music and rhythm are well-received, which matters when someone is in crisis.
by_protocol_plain.H-CR-L2: Harbor is the one protocol in the Healing series that feels like it could be working better. The music ratings are the weakest of any crisis protocol — a notable share found it unremarkable or underwhelming — and some people seem to be using it as a sleep aid rather than a crisis tool, which suggests the routing may need attention. It's not failing, but it's the clearest candidate for a music review in this series.
by_protocol_plain.H-CR-L3: Horizon is interesting: a surprisingly large number of people say they settled almost immediately — within the first minute or two — which is unusually fast for a crisis protocol. On the other hand, a small but notable group said they actively didn't like the music, which is worth keeping an eye on. Overall results are in line with the other crisis protocols, with real improvement being reported.
by_protocol_plain.H-OS-L1: Pebble has the largest listener base of any protocol in the dataset, which makes its results particularly meaningful. It's working broadly well for a wide range of people using it in all kinds of situations — at home, commuting, caregiving. The spread of results is moderate, reflecting that different people in different contexts get different things from it. A reliable, well-evidenced foundation protocol.
by_protocol_plain.H-OS-L2: Rainfall improves on Pebble's mean score and shows consistently positive improvement reports from listeners. It takes a bit longer to settle into — around 10 minutes typically — which makes sense for a longer protocol. The rhythm is landing well across the board, with most people finding it either comfortably balanced or notably calming. A strong mid-tier protocol.
by_protocol_plain.H-OS-L3: Glacier scores highest in the over-stimulation group and has a pleasant surprise: a large number of people are using it to try to sleep, even though it's designed for over-stimulation recovery. It seems to be working reasonably well in that context too, which suggests the descent architecture is genuinely flexible. Worth noting in routing guidance as a secondary sleep option.
by_protocol_plain.H-SL-L1: Lantern is well-liked — almost everyone in the qualitative data who responded said they liked or loved the music, which is the strongest music reception in the Sleep group. Results are positive and consistent with a broad range of direct listeners using it for themselves. Some therapists are also using it in sessions, which is an unusual context for a sleep protocol but doesn't appear to be hurting outcomes.
by_protocol_plain.H-SL-L2: Hammock produces the most consistent results of any protocol in the Healing series — very little variation between people, which is a good sign for reliability. Most of the people reporting on it are using it with or for someone else (caregivers, clinicians), and a few found the rhythm slightly busy in that context. That 'slightly busy' signal is worth investigating — it may not matter much for direct listeners falling asleep, but could be distracting for someone supporting another person.
by_protocol_plain.H-SL-L3: Midnight performs similarly to Hammock and scores well overall. A notable share of listeners found the rhythm 'slightly busy', which is something to watch in a sleep protocol where any sense of complexity could disrupt wind-down. It's not a dominant complaint, but it's more present here than in L1 or L2. Results remain solidly positive.
by_protocol_plain.T-CF-L1: Ember gets people into a creative or focused state reasonably well, but the most common description of change is 'slight improvement' — which may reflect that people starting this protocol are already in a reasonably good place and don't have as far to travel. Many people settle into it quickly, within the first couple of minutes. A functional foundation, but the improvement reports are more modest than in some other Thriving protocols.
by_protocol_plain.T-CF-L2: Current is the most consistent creative flow protocol — very little variation between listeners, and a strong share said they loved the music outright. It works steadily if not dramatically, and the tight results suggest it suits a fairly reliable listener profile. A small number of people listening while driving is an unusual use case worth noting.
by_protocol_plain.T-CF-L3: Aurora is the highest-rated creative flow protocol and the results are impressively consistent — though only 18 people have scored it so far, so we're not ready to draw firm conclusions. A large proportion of listeners are proxies (caregivers, clinicians) rather than direct listeners, and some are using it for sleep rather than creative flow. Despite this, it's performing well. Worth watching closely as the sample grows.
by_protocol_plain.T-FC-L1: Compass gets positive reviews from listeners when they describe their experience in words — many report noticeable or significant improvement. But the rhythm stands out: more people described it as 'slightly busy' than any other protocol in the Thriving series. For a protocol designed to support focus and clarity, a busy-feeling rhythm might be working against some listeners. This is the clearest rhythm adjustment signal in the series.
by_protocol_plain.T-FC-L2: Waypoint is the strongest focus protocol by outcome score and produces good results across a range of listeners. It takes about 10 minutes to settle into, which is typical for this type of protocol. A noticeable number of listeners are using it while supporting someone else, rather than for themselves — which is an interesting use pattern worth understanding better.
by_protocol_plain.T-FC-L3: Summit is the most puzzling protocol in the Thriving series. Outcome scores are the lowest in the Focus group, but when people describe how they felt, the most common response is 'significant improvement' — a genuine mismatch between the two measures. It's not aversive; the rhythm is comfortable and people take a reasonable amount of time to settle. Something about how the experience translates to a number on the scale seems to be breaking down here, and it's worth investigating.
by_protocol_plain.T-MB-L1: Stone is a strong, reliable maintenance protocol — highest-scoring in its group with very consistent results. It's heavily used in therapy sessions, more than almost any other Thriving protocol, and the longer settle time (around 20 minutes) fits that structured clinical context well. Music opinion is mixed rather than warm, which is fine — in a formal therapeutic setting, aesthetic neutrality isn't a problem.
by_protocol_plain.T-MB-L2: Axis shows the most variation in outcomes of any Thriving protocol, meaning some people get a lot from it and others don't respond much. The qualitative data splits almost evenly between direct listeners and clients/patients, which may partly explain that spread. The rhythm is described as 'slightly busy' more often than any other maintenance protocol — something that could be pulling results apart depending on the listener's state and context.
by_protocol_plain.T-MB-L3: Orbit has the highest share of positive outcomes in the entire Thriving series, yet a meaningful number of qualitative respondents say they never settled into it at all. Those two facts can coexist — the people who do connect with it seem to find it genuinely helpful, while others aren't getting there at all. That non-settling signal is unusual for a HOLD protocol and suggests the entry into the hold phase may need adjustment.
by_protocol_plain.X-MC-L1: Cavern is the most musically well-received protocol in the Transforming series — listeners say they liked or loved it more than almost anything else in this series. Outcomes are moderately positive, and people describe settling into it steadily, typically within 5–10 minutes. Two listeners reported feeling worse after using it, which is a rare signal across the whole dataset and worth keeping an eye on.
by_protocol_plain.X-MC-L2: Starlight produces calm, consistent results — tight outcome spread and the strongest 'very settling' rhythm perception in the Transforming series. Most listeners are using it for themselves, and a small number reported no effect, which is worth noting for a protocol aimed at supporting mental clarity. Overall, a quietly solid protocol.
by_protocol_plain.X-MC-L3: Cosmos has only been heard by 10 people so far, which means the results — including a below-average mean score — should be treated as early noise rather than a meaningful signal. A large proportion of responses come from clinicians or caregivers rather than direct listeners, and those who described their experience in words reported mostly positive improvement. It's simply too early to say much. Check back when there are at least 30 outcome scores.
by_protocol_plain.X-MD-L1: Footpath is the entry point for the Motivation and Drive group, targeting listeners in genuinely low-affect states. Outcome scores are modest, but qualitative reports are more positive — many describe noticeable or significant improvement. People settle into it quickly, which is a good sign. The gap between how it scores and how people describe it is consistent with what we'd expect when someone starts from a very low place and finds it harder to give a high number even when things improve.
by_protocol_plain.X-MD-L2: Trail has the lowest positive-outcome rate in the dataset, which is notable. Yet listeners say they love the music — a strong endorsement — and the rhythm feels comfortable. The main issue seems to be time: the modal settle time is 20 minutes, the longest in this group, suggesting it may take longer than the current protocol allows to actually lift people who are in a low-motivation state. An extended version might perform better.
by_protocol_plain.X-MD-L3: Switchback scores well on average, but the results are all over the place — some listeners score it very low, others very high, with less in between. A striking finding is that many people are using it to try to sleep, even though it's designed to lift energy and motivation. Those two use cases don't really fit together, and this mismatch is the most likely reason for the split results. Routing guidance may need to clarify when Switchback is and isn't appropriate.
by_protocol_plain.X-ME-L1: Breeze is the gentlest entry into the Mood and Energy group and produces modest but real results. People love the music — the strongest music enthusiasm in the Transforming series — and the improvement descriptions in qualitative responses are genuinely positive. It takes a while to settle (around 20 minutes), which makes sense for a protocol gently lifting people from a low starting point. Patience is built into how it works.
by_protocol_plain.X-ME-L2: Sunrise shows the strongest positive improvement descriptions of the Mood and Energy group, with 'significant improvement' leading the qualitative responses. Music is well-liked. Settle time splits interestingly between very fast (immediate) and very slow (20 minutes), suggesting there may be two distinct types of listeners using this protocol — one group that clicks with it immediately and one that needs more time, or starts from a lower place.
by_protocol_plain.X-ME-L3: Updraft is generating a lot of listener feedback relative to how many people have scored it, which suggests engaged listeners. The music is well-received, and the rhythm feels appropriate. The main concern is that about a fifth of qualitative respondents report no effect — higher than expected for this group. It may be that some listeners reaching L3 are in states too low for the protocol to lift meaningfully within its current timeframe.
